refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation link availability ≥ 0.999 (99.9%) over the agreed evaluation period, measured as the ratio of time the bidirectional command-and-telemetry link is operational to total evaluation time.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-010 addresses a critical operational requirement: remote operators must reliably command and receive feedback from equipment. The 0.999 threshold (approximately 43 minutes downtime per month) is a standard for safety-critical teleoperation. Refinement clarifies measurement scope (bidirectional link), evaluation period (customer-defined), and the quantitative target.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous monitoring of link state transitions (up/down) during the evaluation period; calculation of cumulative uptime; comparison against 0.999 threshold. Requires instrumentation of Network Link state and timestamped logging.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from Tele-operator to Vehicle.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from Vehicle to Tele-operator.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures and initiate recovery within defined time bounds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics for post-evaluation analysis.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant network paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover; monitor link health continuously; log state transitions.
DP-B: Implement single primary network path with periodic health checks and manual recovery procedures.
DP-C: Implement multi-carrier aggregation (cellular + satellite + terrestrial) with intelligent routing and seamless handoff; continuous monitoring and automatic failover.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Implement multi-carrier aggregation (cellular + satellite + terrestrial) with intelligent routing and seamless handoff; continuous monitoring and automatic failover.

potential_sources: DP-C couples to network infrastructure availability (external dependency on carrier SLAs); mitigation via multi-carrier selection reduces single-point-of-failure risk.
DP-C couples to Vehicle's communication hardware (modem, antenna diversity); mitigation via hardware redundancy and failover logic.
DP-C couples to Tele-operator's network connectivity; mitigation via operator site network design (out of scope for NGHE but acknowledged).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is the least coupled to internal NGHE design choices; it delegates carrier selection and failover logic to a dedicated Teleoperation module. Coupling is primarily to external network infrastructure, which is manageable via SLA selection and multi-carrier strategy.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Multiple carriers (cellular, satellite, terrestrial) are available at the worksite. Risk: Remote or rural sites may have limited carrier coverage; mitigation via pre-site survey and fallback to single-carrier mode.

Assumption: Seamless handoff logic can be implemented without introducing latency that violates operator control responsiveness. Risk: Handoff delays could degrade operator experience; mitigation via latency budgeting and testing.
Assumption: Vehicle hardware supports multi-modem architecture. Risk: Cost and power budget impact; mitigation via design trade study.
Assumption: Continuous monitoring does not introduce excessive overhead. Risk: Monitoring traffic could consume bandwidth; mitigation via efficient heartbeat protocol.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: DP-C is sound but should be refined to specify: (1) carrier selection criteria (coverage, latency, cost), (2) failover decision logic (e.g., signal strength thresholds), (3) monitoring heartbeat interval and timeout thresholds, (4) graceful degradation strategy if fewer than 2 carriers are available.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the multi-carrier aggregation logic, failover controller, link health monitor, and telemetry logger. Interfaces with Vehicle (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert). Responsible for achieving the 0.999 availability target through intelligent routing and redundancy management.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands from Tele-operator; Teleoperation module routes commands to Vehicle via selected carrier.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Sends vehicle state and sensor data from Vehicle to Tele-operator; Teleoperation module aggregates and forwards telemetry via selected carrier.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services; Teleoperation module prioritizes safety alerts in failover logic. May send link-failure alerts to Safety Services.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages physical/logical network connections (cellular, satellite, terrestrial modems); Teleoperation module controls modem selection, monitors signal quality, and triggers failover.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning IM to NGHE Solution would diffuse link availability responsibility across all sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), violating cohesion. Not recommended.

Vehicle owns the physical communication hardware (modems, antennas) but does not own the multi-carrier routing logic or failover controller. Assigning IM to Vehicle would couple vehicle control logic to network management, violating separation of concerns. Partial containment only.

Teleoperation is the natural owner of link availability. It owns the multi-carrier aggregation logic, failover controller, link health monitor, and telemetry logger. All DP-C elements (routing, failover, monitoring) are cohesively contained. Minimal diffusion to external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link).

Safety Services owns safety-critical logic (e.g., emergency stop, hazard detection) but not network management. Assigning IM to Safety Services would conflate safety logic with network availability, violating single responsibility. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of link availability. It naturally encapsulates multi-carrier routing, failover logic, and link health monitoring. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. Containment score 0.95 reflects high cohesion and low diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Teleoperation module may become a bottleneck if link monitoring and failover logic are not efficiently implemented. Mitigation: Design for low-latency failover (target <100 ms) and use asynchronous monitoring.

Risk: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle's communication hardware (modems, antennas). If hardware fails, DP-C cannot compensate. Mitigation: Ensure Vehicle provides redundant modem hardware; Teleoperation logic assumes hardware redundancy.

Risk: Teleoperation module depends on external carriers (cellular, satellite, terrestrial). If all carriers fail simultaneously, link availability target cannot be met. Mitigation: Pre-site survey to confirm carrier availability; fallback to single-carrier mode if necessary.

Risk: SafetyAlert interface may introduce coupling between link availability and safety logic. Mitigation: Define clear contract for SafetyAlert (e.g., link failure triggers safety alert, but safety logic does not dictate failover strategy).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Teleoperation module assignment is sound. Recommend clarifying: (1) Hardware assumptions (Vehicle must provide redundant modems), (2) Carrier SLA requirements (minimum uptime per carrier), (3) Failover decision logic (e.g., signal strength thresholds, timeout values), (4) Monitoring overhead budget (e.g., heartbeat interval, telemetry logging frequency).

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (multi-carrier aggregation with intelligent routing and seamless handoff) directly addresses FR-NGHE-010 (achieve 0.999 link availability). The DP is specific, measurable, and technically feasible. Multi-carrier redundancy is a proven approach for high-availability teleoperation systems. No FR-DP mismatch detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle's communication hardware. If hardware design is not finalized, Teleoperation DP may need revision. Mitigation: Establish hardware requirements early (e.g., dual modems, antenna diversity).
Teleoperation module depends on external carriers. Carrier availability is outside NGHE control. Mitigation: Pre-site survey and fallback strategy.

Teleoperation module interfaces with Safety Services via SafetyAlert. If Safety Services logic changes, Teleoperation failover strategy may need adjustment. Mitigation: Define clear SafetyAlert contract.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module achieves high containment (0.95) of DP-C. Multi-carrier routing, failover logic, and link health monitoring are cohesively owned. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. DP-IM alignment is strong.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-010 is verifiable via continuous monitoring of link state and calculation of uptime ratio. Teleoperation module can instrument link state transitions and log metrics for post-evaluation analysis. Verification is feasible and recommended.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: FR-NGHE-010 is clearly allocated to Teleoperation module. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-010.1 through 010.4) are allocated to Teleoperation and Vehicle (for hardware support). Allocation is clear and traceable.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-C to specify carrier selection criteria, failover decision logic, monitoring heartbeat interval, and graceful degradation strategy.
Establish hardware requirements for Vehicle (dual modems, antenna diversity) to support Teleoperation DP.
Define SafetyAlert contract between Teleoperation and Safety Services to clarify failover-safety interaction.
Plan for pre-site survey to confirm carrier availability and establish fallback strategy for single-carrier mode.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-010 is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (command link, telemetry link, failure detection/recovery, metrics logging). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection. Teleoperation module assignment is sound and ready for next level. Recommend decomposing sub-FRs and assigning DPs for each.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-010.1: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional command link from Tele-operator to Vehicle with latency ≤ [TBD] ms.
FR-NGHE-010.2: The teleoperation system shall establish and maintain a bidirectional telemetry link from Vehicle to Tele-operator with latency ≤ [TBD] ms.
FR-NGHE-010.3: The teleoperation system shall detect link failures within [TBD] seconds and initiate recovery (failover to alternate carrier) within [TBD] seconds.
FR-NGHE-010.4: The teleoperation system shall log link availability metrics (uptime, downtime, failover events, carrier selection) for post-evaluation analysis.

revise.why: DP-C should be refined to specify: (1) carrier selection criteria (coverage, latency, cost), (2) failover decision logic (signal strength thresholds, timeout values), (3) monitoring heartbeat interval and timeout thresholds, (4) graceful degradation strategy if fewer than 2 carriers are available. Current DP is directionally correct but lacks implementation detail.

assumptions: Multiple carriers (cellular, satellite, terrestrial) are available at the worksite.
Vehicle hardware supports multi-modem architecture with antenna diversity.
Seamless handoff logic can be implemented without introducing unacceptable latency.
Continuous monitoring does not introduce excessive bandwidth overhead.
Tele-operator site has reliable network connectivity (out of scope for NGHE).
Safety Services logic does not dictate failover strategy; failover is transparent to safety logic.
open_risks: Remote or rural sites may have limited carrier coverage; pre-site survey required.
Handoff delays could degrade operator experience; latency budgeting and testing required.
Vehicle hardware cost and power budget impact; design trade study required.
Monitoring overhead could consume bandwidth; efficient heartbeat protocol required.
All carriers fail simultaneously; fallback to single-carrier mode or manual recovery required.
evidence_needed: Carrier availability map for target worksites.
Vehicle hardware design (modem specifications, antenna configuration).
Latency budget for operator control responsiveness.
Monitoring protocol design and overhead analysis.
Failover decision logic and timeout thresholds.
Test plan for multi-carrier failover scenarios.
Post-evaluation link availability metrics and analysis.
